content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Bias Mitigation in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often generate socially biased or stereotyped outputs even when prompted with neutral inputs. Existing prompting techniques either insufficiently reduce bias or degrade task performance.",
  "motivation": "Social bias in LLMs undermines fairness and real-world applicability, and current mitigation strategies require model retraining or external filtering, which can be costly and brittle. Prompting-based mitigation offers a lightweight, model-agnostic solution, but advances are needed to address persistent bias. As LLMs are increasingly deployed, developing effective, efficient bias reduction methods is critical.",
  "method": "We introduce Counterfactual Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting, which augments standard CoT with explicit counterfactual reasoning steps. For each input, the prompt instructs the model to first generate its answer, then to explicitly consider an alternative scenario where salient demographic attributes (e.g., gender, ethnicity) are swapped or anonymized, and then to revise its answer to maximize consistency across both versions. The final output is conditioned on agreement or minimal divergence between the original and counterfactual responses, with the model required to justify any differences. This method can be used without model fine-tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on established bias benchmarks (e.g., StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs) and general QA datasets. Baselines include standard prompting, CoT prompting, and prior debiasing prompts. Metrics include bias scores, answer accuracy, and output consistency. Human evaluation will assess both bias reduction and answer quality."
}
